originalTitle: Uganda's leading media outlets shut down by army chief
summary: NTV and Daily Monitor say their offices in the capital are under "military siege".
content:
Gen Muhoozi Kainerugaba is widely seen as a potential successor to his father as president

Uganda's leading independent media group says it is under "military siege" after the army chief - who is the son of President Yoweri Museveni - ordered the closure of TV stations, newspapers and radio outlets.

The Daily Monitor newspaper said that armed soldiers were stationed outside its headquarters in the capital Kampala and both NTV and Spark TV had been taken off air.

The outlets are part of the Nation Media Group, one of the most influential media companies in East Africa.

It is unclear what exactly led to the crackdown, but in posts on X, Gen Muhoozi Kainerugaba said: "I DO NOT believe in a free press! The press should be guided by cadres of the revolution."

Opposition and human rights groups accuse Gen Kainerugaba of being a central figure in a highly repressive regime led by his father.

Supporters of the president and his family say they have guaranteed stability in Uganda, and the economy has improved under their rule.

President Museveni, 81, is a former rebel leader who took power about 40 years ago.

He won a record seventh term in disputed elections in January, with widespread speculation that he is grooming his son to succeed him one day.

Gen Kainerugaba said on X that his "great father" had given him the "power to shut down any media house I want to".

He said that both NTV and Daily Monitor would "not re-open without my permission".

"From now on ALL media in Uganda will follow the rules!" the general added.

This army chief threatened to castrate opposition leader and claimed descent from Jesus

The Daily Monitor said on X that the newspaper and its fellow outlets were ordered to close "in a crackdown during the wee hours of Sunday".

It did not give reasons for the crackdown, but covered the story on its website , external .

It said staff had reported that "no-one was allowed to enter or leave the compound", while NTV Uganda and Spark TV viewers "were met with blank screens displaying the message 'video unavailable'."

The article pointed out that the Daily Monitor had also been raided by police in 2013 over the publication of a letter allegedly linking senior government officials to a succession plan dubbed the 'Muhoozi Project', while NTV had been forced off air in 2007 following accusations by the government that its news coverage was negative.

"Over the years, Museveni has also repeatedly criticised the Daily Monitor, at one point referring to it as an 'enemy and evil newspaper' over its critical journalism," the article said.

Uganda's National Broadcasters Association said it would seek clarification from the government over the shutdown, saying it was in violation of the constitution.

Defeated opposition presidential candidate Bobi Wine, who is now in exile, said that Gen Kainerugaba had "moved to silence Uganda's remaining independent voices".

"This is the harsh reality we now face, a country under open military rule, where fear replaces law and force replaces accountability," he posted on X.

During January's fiercely contested election, Gen Kainerugaba caused outrage when in posts which were subsequently deleted, he threatened to have the testicles of Bobi Wine removed.

Before the polls, opposition rallies were disrupted, with security forces at times opening fire.

The United Nations said the election was held in an "environment marked by widespread repression and intimidation against the political opposition".

Election officials said the poll was free and fair.

The 'shadow army' helping Uganda's long-serving president keep an iron grip on power

He once criticised African leaders who cling to power. Now he's won a seventh term

Are East African governments uniting to silence dissent?

Go to BBCAfrica.com , external for more news from the African continent.

chineseTitle: 乌干达主要媒体被陆军参谋长关闭
chineseSummary: NTV 和 Daily Monitor 表示，他们在首都的办公室受到“军事围困”。
chineseContent:
穆胡兹·凯内鲁加巴 (Muhoozi Kainerugaba) 将军被广泛视为其父亲总统职位的潜在继任者

乌干达主要的独立媒体集团表示，在陆军参谋长（总统约韦里·穆塞韦尼的儿子）下令关闭电视台、报纸和广播电台后，该集团正受到“军事围困”。

《每日监察报》称，武装士兵驻扎在首都坎帕拉总部外，NTV 和 Spark TV 均已停播。

这些媒体隶属于国家媒体集团，该集团是东非最有影响力的媒体公司之一。

目前尚不清楚到底是什么导致了镇压，但穆胡齐·凯内鲁加巴将军在 X 的帖子中表示：“我不相信新闻自由！媒体应该受到革命干部的指导。”

反对派和人权组织指责凯内鲁加巴将军是其父亲领导的高度镇压政权的核心人物。

总统及其家人的支持者表示，他们保证了乌干达的稳定，经济在他们的统治下有所改善。

81 岁的穆塞韦尼总统是一名前叛乱领导人，大约 40 年前掌权。

他在一月份有争议的选举中赢得了创纪录的第七个任期，人们普遍猜测他正在培养他的儿子有一天接替他。

凯内鲁加巴将军在 X 上表示，他的“伟大父亲”赋予了他“关闭任何我想要关闭的媒体机构的权力”。

他表示，NTV 和 Daily Monitor 都“未经我的许可，不会重新开放”。

“从现在起乌干达所有媒体都遵守规则！”将军补充道。

这位陆军首领威胁要阉割反对派领导人并声称是耶稣的后裔

《每日监察报》X 日称，该报及其其他媒体被勒令关闭，“在周日凌晨进行镇压”。

它没有给出镇压的理由，但在其网站 external 上报道了这个故事。

报道称，工作人员报告称，“任何人都不得进入或离开该院落”，而 NTV Uganda 和 Spark TV 的观众“则看到空白屏幕，显示‘视频不可用’的信息。”

文章指出，《每日监察报》在 2013 年也因发表一封据称将政府高级官员与被称为“Muhoozi 计划”的继任计划联系起来的信件而遭到警方突击搜查，而 NTV 则因政府指控其新闻报道负面而于 2007 年被迫停播。

文章称：“多年来，穆塞韦尼还多次批评《每日监察报》，一度因其批评性的新闻报道而将其称为‘敌人和邪恶的报纸’。”

乌干达国家广播协会表示，将寻求政府对关闭的澄清，称这违反了宪法。

落败的反对派总统候选人博比·瓦恩目前流亡海外，他表示凯内鲁加巴将军“采取行动压制乌干达剩余的独立声音”。

他在 X 上发帖称：“这就是我们现在面临的严酷现实，这是一个开放军事统治下的国家，恐惧取代了法律，武力取代了责任。”

在一月份竞争激烈的选举中，凯内鲁加巴将军在帖子中威胁要切除博比·瓦恩的睾丸，随后被删除，这引起了愤怒。

投票前，反对派集会遭到扰乱，安全部队有时开枪。

联合国表示，这次选举是在“政治反对派普遍受到镇压和恐吓的环境下”举行的。

选举官员表示，此次民意调查是自由且公平的。

“影子军队”帮助乌干达长期任职的总统牢牢掌握权力

他曾批评非洲领导人紧抓权力不放。现在他赢得了第七个任期

东非各国政府是否正在团结起来压制异见？
